planting.note: Plant fresh seeds in spring or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; anacua is a Texas native of the Rio Grande Plains and Gulf Coast that is semi-evergreen and tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and coastal conditions well.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; anacua is a native south Texas and Mexico tree of the Rio Grande Plains and Gulf Coast, valued for its multi-season ornamental interest: fragrant white flowers in spring, followed by clusters of small orange-yellow fruits (edible, slightly sweet) in late spring and early summer that attract numerous birds. The rough, sandpaper-textured leaves give the tree its secondary common name. The fruit is eaten by numerous bird species, particularly golden-fronted woodpeckers, white-tipped doves, and mockingbirds. Semi-evergreen in mild winters in zone 9a; may lose some leaves in hard freezes but re-leafs readily from the base or branch tips. An excellent, underused native tree for south and central Texas gardens providing year-round interest, wildlife value, and adapting well to the alkaline soils of the Edwards Plateau and lower Rio Grande valley.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after removing the fleshy seed coat and scarifying; soak 24 hours before planting in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun. Western soapberry grows naturally on dry, rocky, calcareous slopes and plains across the south-central US.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous to sandy soil in full sun; western soapberry is a native tree of the south-central US, growing on dry, rocky, calcareous plains and hills from Kansas and Missouri south to Mexico. The fleshy, translucent, golden-amber berries contain saponins — natural soap compounds — and are high enough in saponin concentration that the crushed berries lather in water and have been used historically as soap for washing clothes and hair by indigenous peoples. The fruits are technically toxic if eaten in quantity due to the saponins. The pale yellow-green fall foliage, persistent golden berries through winter, and handsome pinnate leaves provide multi-season ornamental interest. Excellent urban tree tolerating drought, heat, alkaline soils, and neglect — one of the toughest native trees for harsh, dry, calcareous landscape conditions in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, calcareous or sandy soil in full sun; wild tamarind grows naturally in the subtropical hammocks and rocky pinelands of south Florida and the Florida Keys, thriving in the thin, rocky, alkaline soils over oolitic limestone typical of south Florida.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, thin, rocky, calcareous soil in full sun; wild tamarind is a striking native tree of subtropical south Florida, the Florida Keys, and the Caribbean, producing an exceptionally graceful, lacy, filtered-shade canopy of bipinnately compound leaves that resemble tamarind foliage. The tree forms a high, open canopy that provides filtered light — one of the best native trees for creating dappled shade in south Florida landscapes without completely blocking light. Small, fragrant, white pompom flowers in spring attract numerous pollinators. A nitrogen-fixing legume (like true tamarind) that improves soil fertility. The flat, papery seed pods persist through the dry season. Butterfly gardeners prize it as a host plant for large orange sulphur butterfly larvae. The finest native shade tree for zone 10 rocky, calcareous, limestone soil conditions where other trees struggle.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; princess tree grows extremely fast (up to 15 feet per year) and is invasive in the eastern US — listed as invasive in many states. Plant only in contained situations or where it can be managed; do not plant near natural areas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun, with caution regarding invasive potential; princess tree is a Chinese ornamental tree that has become invasive across the eastern and southern United States, spreading prodigiously by wind-dispersed seeds into disturbed habitats, roadsides, riverbanks, and forest margins. The spring flower display is extraordinary — large, erect panicles of lavender-purple, foxglove-shaped flowers bloom in April before the very large, velvety, heart-shaped leaves emerge, creating a spectacular early spring display. The wood is prized in Japan as the finest, lightest, most resonant wood for musical instruments and traditional craft; female dolls and furniture are traditionally made from paulownia wood. Despite its beauty, plant responsibly — avoid locations near natural areas and never plant where it could escape cultivation into native habitats.
sources: USDA Forest Service Invasive Plants List

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, deep, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral riparian soil in full sun; plains cottonwood is the dominant riparian tree of the Great Plains, forming gallery forests along rivers and streams in the otherwise treeless prairie landscape. It grows extremely fast and requires consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral riparian soil in full sun; plains cottonwood is the subspecies of eastern cottonwood (Populus deltoides) adapted to the semi-arid Great Plains, forming the iconic riparian forest galleries along the Platte, Republican, Arkansas, and other prairie rivers of the central US. In the treeless expanses of the Great Plains, cottonwood groves along rivers are biological oases — the only substantial tree cover across thousands of square miles of grassland. They support nesting red-tailed hawks, great horned owls, heron colonies, orioles, and dozens of other bird species that simply cannot exist without tree canopy in the open prairie. The brilliant yellow fall foliage is a spectacular landmark in an otherwise somber fall landscape. The female trees produce cottony seed release in early summer that can be messy — male plants are preferred in urban settings.
